content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Two pages fetch the exact same amount of data, parse it in the exact same amount of time, and render the same list. One feels instant. The other feels slow enough that a user reaches for the back button before it’s done. Every phase in both pages takes exactly as long as the other page’s equivalent phase. The only thing that’s different between them is the order those phases happen in.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

waterfallPress enter or click to view image in full size

lightoust performance metricsWhat we actually mean by feeling slow
There’s a specific moment we’re chasing when we talk about a page feeling slow, even if we don’t usually name it directly. Time to Interactive is the point where a page can actually respond to something a user does, a click registers, a scroll works, not just the point where something becomes visible on screen.
These two moments aren’t the same thing, and the gap between them is exactly where sequencing lives. A page can paint something early and still not be interactive, if the thing on screen isn’t wired up yet, or if the main thread is still busy with something else entirely. A page can also delay painting anything at all for reasons that have nothing to do with how fast the work underneath actually is…
